# Transcrição: 18-Kelly in the Toilet

- **Fonte:** 18-Kelly in the Toilet
- **Duração:** 07:21
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:01]

I'm only eight years old, but I already know my sister is in trouble when I look at her. At the bottom of the driveway, we're waiting at the bus stop underneath the big oak tree. My brother's there too. He's Jeremy and he's seven years old. And then my sister Kelly, she's five. And she's doing that dance, that dance that little girls do when they have to pee. And even at this young age, I understand how girls work, little girls at least. I know that their feet know they have to pee before their brains know they have to pee. And so I know this is going to be a problem because the bus is coming and she's going to ask me in a minute to pee. And I'm not going to be able to take her to pee because the bus is coming. But I know that's not going to work either because the bus ride is 25 minutes. And I know that it's a bumpy ride and it's going to be problematic if I get her on the bus. And I'm sort of trapped in this middle ground looking at my cute little sister. She's tiny. She like fits in the palm of my hand with her, her little pink dress and pigtails. And I'm just trying to figure out what to do when she turns to me and says, Matt, I have to pee. And I say, no, you can't pee. But I know that's not going to work. And at that moment, Jeremy tells me, no, it's fine. And I turn and I look and he's lying in the road with his ear on the pavement. He's an idiot. And we know that. My brother and we all know he's an idiot. He's like shy beyond belief. He's got glasses that have been broken and they've got medical tape holding them together. And he wrote a J on the medical tape because he thinks that makes him a superhero. And he believes that he can put his ear on the pavement and hear the bus. And my sister and I are terrible to him, but we've always given him this one thing, like we let him have it. And so he says, it's fine. I know it's not coming. And I say, OK, I'll bring you to pee. And we run up to the house and we're latchkey kids where there's no one home. So I have to get in the house. My mom ties a key around my neck every morning. And because I lost it once, she ties it tight. So I can't get it off. Like it will kill me someday if I get caught on a chain link or something like that. But now when I open the door, I have to sort of squat to the door. And I have to line up my neck with the lock and then lean in and turn it. But I'm good at it because I do it every single day. So I get the door open and I say, Kelly, run and pee and come back. And then I turn and I lock the door and pull it. So when then she leaves at a lock behind her and I go in and wait with Jeremy, who is still lying in the road with his ear to the pavement, which is not great because this is like a 45 mile an hour road. Not a lot of cars come, but whatever. So I'm waiting and staring at the door and one minute becomes two and two becomes three. And soon it's five minutes. And I'm wondering where the hell is Kelly? So I tell Jeremy, I say, I'm going to go get Kelly. If the bus comes, tell them to wait for us. And so I run up back to the house and I get down again to open the door. And I get the door open and when I throw it open, I can hear her crying. And I know something bad has happened. And so I run through the kitchen and through the dining room and down the hall. And just to the left is the bathroom. And when I get to the bathroom, I can hear she's really upset.

## [03:01 - 06:02]

And my hand hits the knob and starts to turn and she screams and she says, don't open the door. And I say, what happened? And she said, don't open the door. And I said, we need to get on the bus. And she says, don't open the door. And I'm just imagining blood on the other side of this door. Something terrible has happened. And I'm looking around, wishing there was an adult and not an eight-year-old boy, which is all I am. But I realize I have to go through this door. So I push it open and I look and I cannot believe what I see. Kelly is completely naked. Now, I only know this because her dress and her underwear are on the floor because I really can't see her. Because almost all of her is inside the toilet. And her rush to jump onto the toilet, she jumped into the toilet. And so I can only see her feet and her head and everything else is below the rim. And she's just crying, that snotty, heaving cry that little kids do. And I don't know why she's naked. Like, apparently she takes her clothes off when she goes to the bathroom, which is new to me. And she says, don't look at me. And so I close my eyes and I say, I need to get you out of the toilet. So I'll keep my eyes closed. And I feel for her. And when I get to her, I get my hands under her armpits. And I pull her and she pops like a cork. And I can hear the splash. She was like in the water. And so she's out and I've still got my eyes closed. And I say, we have to go on. She says, I have to pee. And I'm like, God damn it. So I say, pee and get your clothes on. And I'll wait outside. And so I go right outside the door and I'm standing there just looking around, really thinking, I'm not supposed to be the oldest person in the house right now. Like, why am I the adult? And I'm always this way. Like, my parents go out on Saturday nights to box seats until 2 in the morning to get drunk. I am the person who has to sit on the couch and not fall asleep until they get home babysitting. And I'm constantly throughout my entire life thinking, they're supposed to be someone older than me around. And it's always me. And so finally, Kelly's ready. And I give her credit. She got she was fast. And she's got her dress on. And we run to the door and we throw it open. And we start down the stairs. And I look down at the driveway. And my brother is not there. And I realize what's happened. The idiot is so shy that he couldn't tell the bus driver to wait for us. He just got on the bus and left us. And so I go back into the house. And I call my mother. She's a pharmacist at the hospital. And I say, ma, Kelly fell into the toilet. And she couldn't get out. And Jeremy didn't tell the bus driver to stop. And now we're stuck here in the house. And I don't know what to do. And she says, calm down. Tell me what happened. And I tell her slowly what happened. And then there's that pregnant parental pause where she wonders, like, how bad of a decision can I make and not end up in jail? And she says, stay home. Lock the door. And I turn to my sister and I say, it's a snow day, but there's no snow. And it's amazing because we have the house to ourselves. And we pour Kool-Aid on Wheaties just to see what it's like and put peanut butter

## [06:02 - 07:21]

on everything. And we watch Days of Our Lives and All My Children. And we prank every single business in the town. Phoning all afternoon. It's great. And I can't wait to tell my friends. And so the next day, we manage to get on the bus. And there's a boy on the bus named Henry Savoy. He's an old boy. And I want to be friends with him. He's kind of a criminal already. And I go to the back of the bus. And I say, Henry, you're not going to believe what happened yesterday. And I tell him, the eight-year-old and the five-year-old were home all alone, all day long. And I'll never forget the look on his face. I think he's going to be excited for me. And the look on his face is, that is fucked up. Like this future legitimate criminal who's probably already a felon, he knows that an eight-year-old and a five-year-old are not supposed to be home alone. And like that, the delusion is broken. Like, I suddenly understand that the family I live in is not entirely normal. And I'm not supposed to be the oldest person in the house all the time. And I'm not supposed to be the person who's taking responsibility for all the things that I am. And this felon on the back of the bus knows it. And my mother and my father don't. Thank you. Thank you.
